content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Inside Llewyn Davis (2013)

**Score:** 31
**Body:** It’s not a niche movie or unknown but I highly recommend watching it or just rewatching it. I think it’s one of the very few flawless movies that have been made. 

It hits more if you like folk music/bob dylan but I think it is the counterpart to the Coen Brother’s The Big Lebowski in the sense that both are just absolutely flawless in what they are doing. 

Always thought it was the only “Bob Dylan Biopic” ever needed (although not about Bob Dylan but just about the Gaslight Folk scene of the time) but I also liked A Complete Unknown.

Also really great and funny John Goodman performance. “This would interest you.”

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'm not seeing money here!
2. I liked it.  I thought all the actors were great. And I love that style.
3. Oscar really nailed the vocals as well. Best version of Shoals of Herring that I have ever heard. Great movie.
4. I watch it multiple times a year, it’s one of my comfort movies. 
5. Tangentially related to this movie — as like a wrap party kind of thing, they had a special on maybe HBO, I forget now it’s been a while, where they had a bunch of different folk and bluegrass musicians perform the songs from the movie live on stage. It was so good. I’d love to find that again and rewatch. 
  - “Another Day, Another Time” - a documentary about a concert they had to celebrate the music that was in the movie. Fantastic doc. 
6. So good
7. What is your take on the cyclical ending? Who is the man in black? Why the cat?
8. Too funny you mention this film. It came up in a YT vid I watched yesterday about "Cat Acting" - literally 23 minutes on how they get Cats to act in films lol.
9. my favorite part is the irony of llewyn singing a beautiful, flawless song to modest reaction from the audience. then we see dylan getting ready to perform in the background. and we all know bob cant sing and yet he becomes enormously successful while llewyn slips into obscurity. those kinds of observations in coen bros movies is why i love them. they’re sometimes goofy, but if you look close, they’re smart as hell

## 2. Yo guys, i'm looking for some fun/horror movies.

**Score:** 23
**Body:** I feel like watching something horror but fun at the same time, something like: Near Dark (1987) ; The Blob (1988) ; Bone Tomahawk (2015) ; Vampires (1998) ; Bug (2006). I should mention that i'm a little bit drunk and in a big mood of watching a fun horror movie, i have some chips, a sandwich and i'm ready, all i need is a good movie.

I decided to watch: The Wolf of Snow Hollow 2020. ( i feel like this one is going to make me feel good and put me in a good mood ) \[after i watched it i can say that it was ok, had some funny moments and it kinda filled my need and my mood, i liked it\] thank you for the recommendation, [humboldtree](https://www.reddit.com/user/humboldtree/) :D 

I just finished watching Hatchet 2006 and this one was really really good and i really enjoyed it, right up my alley. thank you for this great recommendation, [DiamondContent2011](https://www.reddit.com/user/DiamondContent2011/) :D

but i will still want to see a lot of movies so every recommendation is more than welcome, i love you guys so much and i appreciate every recommendation, thank you! i love this community so much!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A Cabin in the Woods
2. An American Werewolf in London (1981)

Fright Night (1985)
3. Shawn of the dead
4. Tucker and Dale vs Evil, The Babysitter, Evil Dead (the original).
  - Tucker and Dale is the funniest!
5. you should check “the lost boys” it’s perfect for this 
6. What They do in the Shadows (2014)
7. Teeth
8. *The Frighteners (1996)*
9. Weapons

## 3. Laugh out loud comedy in recent years

**Score:** 23
**Body:** Any suggestions on recent comedies that are simply stupid good fun?

No horror themes, no gore, no social commentary, just silliness.

Comedies in recent years always seem to be doing comedy PLUS…. a bunch of other stuff. Sometimes forgetting to be a comedy at all.

My friend just wants to laugh and forget his troubles for 1.5 hours.

The only movie in recent memory that he happens to enjoy is Please Don't Destroy: The Treasure of Foggy Mountain (2023).

I liked Vacation Friends (2021) and showed it to him. He found it funny but is always asking me for lighter comedies like Foggy Mountain.

For more reference: Older movies he likes are Liar Liar, Coming to America, and Trading Places…40 Year Old Virgin, Anchorman.. But I am looking for movies that came out in *recent years.*

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Not very recent but a lot of people haven’t seen Spy.  It’s a gem.
2. I would definitely say Tropic Thunder 2008 is up there as one of the all time comedies you should see.

And another popular cult comedy Idiocracy 2006.

Don't know how recent you'd say is recent but out of the last 26 years these two standout in my mind when you say comedy.
3. naked gun (2025), ted 2, everything everywhere all at once
4. Palm Springs with Andy Samburg. It's on Hulu. It's a different twist on Groundhog Day. 
5. Game Night 10/10 good time 
6. Renfield was really funny but yea they definitely don’t make comedy like they used to
7. We're the Millers 2013.  this movie si soooooo goooood bro trust me, comedy and good vibes.🤘🍻
8. The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent (2023)

Barb and Star Go to Vista Del Mar (2021)

Fackham Hall (2025)

Snack Shack (2024)

Roofman (2025)
9. Ricky Stanicky
10. Friendship!

## 4. Films set in the desert or desert towns with an element of culty sleaze

**Score:** 21
**Body:** Exact genre doesn't really matter so much, but I'm looking for pulpy culty sleazy films set in desolate dry locations (or the towns near the outskirts). Guns, drugs, sex, Elvis impersonators...general celebration of lowbrow film.  
&nbsp;  
I'd prefer to stay away from "generally desolate", so Fargo doesn't really have the right dusty quality to it. An element of dry and hot seems to factor into the vibe I'm after. If a low twangy baritone guitar could fit in somewhere, it probably fits the bill (even if that specific sound/instrument isn't used at all).

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. U-Turn (1997)
  - First movie I thought of
2. Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas
3. Wake in Fright
4. From Dusk Till Dawn
5. Tremors
6. Rango
7. Duel
8. The Salton Sea - 2002

Covers a lot of real estate from the Coachella valley to downtown Los Angeles to high desert 😵‍💫
9. One of my favorite forgotten favorites from the 90's....Breakdown (1997).


For some reason we ditched school at lunch so we could catch an afternoon showing at the mall.  I never wanted the bad guy to die as much as I did in this.  At a certain point you kinda even want his wife and kid to go with him.  

## 5. Movies with mother daughter relationships

**Score:** 21
**Body:** What the title says. 

But not with toxic mothers. A realistic mother daughter relationship. Where it’s evident that they love each other but both make mistakes. 

Kinda like LadyBird

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Steel Magnolias
2. Everything everywhere all at once
3. Hope Floats (1998)
4. Turning Red
5. Stepmom 
6. Joy Luck Club, and bring tissues 
7. Freaky Friday (2003) 🤣
8. Mermaids


9. Postcards from the Edge... Kinda. 

Terms of Endearment
 
Are You There God, It's Me Margaret 
10. Little Women (my favorite is the 1994 version)

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Brendan Gleeson turns 71 today. Happy birthday to such a great actor!

**Score:** 4470

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It's rare to see someone who can do both intimate indie films and massive blockbusters without losing what makes them compelling.
  - It also reminds me of William Dafoe who played in movies of all genres (from superhero movies to arthouse cinema) and almost all famous directors: Wes Anderson, Lars von Trier, Martin Scorsese, Robert Eggers, Oliver Stone, Yorgos Lantimos, Sam Raimi 😆
2. The Guard isn’t there?
  - The Guard epitomises the movie that hardly anyone has seen but that everyone who has loves. 
    - You know, I can't tell if you're really motherfuckin' dumb, or really motherfuckin' smart.
3. He steals the show everytime he is on Screen. 

4. Absolute legend & a national treasure.🙌🇮🇪
5. Braveheart

Troy

Gangs of New York

Favourite films of his for me.
6. Making a list of the best Brendan Gleeson performances is difficult because, as far as I can tell, there's not a single performance by him that wasn't incredible. One of the very few actors whom, if he's in it, I'm watching it, no questions asked. If you really want to see how great this guy is, check out "The Guard" (2011). As black Irish comedies go, this one's vantablack, and was written and directed by John Michael McDonagh ("Calvary"), brother of Martin McDonagh.
7. Kingdom of heaven too

## 2. Favorite most terrifying realistic villain?

**Score:** 928
**Body:** I have just watched City of God for the first time. Great movie. Lil Ze is a fantastic villain with amazing character development. The fact that he’s realistic adds to the level of terror which Ze translates. 

Any more terrifying REALISTIC villains? 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. City of God is Probably one of my favorite movies of all time. Great acting, great cinematography, perfectly paced. I have watched it at least 50x
2. Tom Cruise’s Vincent in Collateral was a brilliant depiction of a former Delta Force operative turned contract killer. That is what I imagine a Jocko Willink or Jay Cal type individual would be like if they went fully rogue.

[Yo homie is that my briefcase?](https://youtu.be/wpdFUueS9xM?si=bwX4_K_QbFpQwu3P).
3. Marlo Stanfield in the Wire 
  - Chris parlow too. He was just so cold.
    - I think Chris scares me more! You may be able to cut a deal with Marlo if you have something to offer.  If Chris comes your dead man 
4. Denzel in Training Days is probably my favourite
5. John Lithgow as a serial killer. 


Or Emilio Lizardo. 
6. Forest Whitaker in Last King of Scotland
7. nicky santoro Casino
8. Jesse Eisenberg as Mark Zuckerberg in “The Social Network.”

## 3. For those who watched 300 in theaters in 2006, what was the experience like? Its distinct visual style and goosebump-inducing scenes must have been something on the big screen.

**Score:** 646

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes, it was amazing. The tropes of that movie were repeated so often, but this was the original.
2. It was a pretty incredible experience honestly. Much of the visuals were state of the art at the time and while this movie didn’t pioneer green screen technology, it furthered its progression immensely seemingly overnight. A movie had never been made relying solely on green screen technology before 300. It showcased whats possible with state of the art (at the time) visual effects tech. 

Again, 300 didn’t pioneer the tech and wasn’t groundbreaking or anything but they did stretch tech in new ways paving the way for what we now know to be normal cinema.
  - And they used green screen to great effect. The movie has a weird, almost dreamlike feel to it, which makes it look incredibly unique.

Now Marvel just uses green screen to put an actor in a freaking apartment room, instead of actually filming there or in a studio.
3. I vividly remember the first trailer I saw in a theater.

For context - I had never heard of the comic, but rather was a history buff that read about the 10 thousand and the hot gates as a child and loved all these heroic stories from ancient Greece, both historical and mythological.

So I'm sitting in a cinaplex as the lights dim, munching on popcorn as I rock the seat back and forth trying to settle in, its not silent - there's too many people for that - but its a good crowd with decent etiquette.  I just gotten comfortable when the darkness was broken by a smash cut of Leonidas and the Spartans driving the persians over the cliff edge. The king's black horsehair crest waving above his helmet, the slow motion twisting forms of the medes vassal soldiers falling to the sea and rocks below, set against a glowing sepia sky.

My breath was taken away at the sheer joyful nerdiness of it.  It was history that other people had looked at me weird if I brought it up, but made to look amazing.  It was like watching the incrediblely detailed illustrations of these old set of 50s or 60s history books that I had read by flashlight as I sat in my closet fort as a child just come to glorious life.

My friend told me I had just sat there with a hand in my mouth, frozen in place.

Its one of my favorite memories of going to the movies.

https://preview.redd.it/vq54qs6f6yrg1.jpeg?width=602&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=530c344ce8051c664db72ac48c35f4f25d2734f2
  - I must have watched that trailer 500 times!!! 

And shared it with Anyone that would listen!! When the movie finally released and the credits rolled i just sat there in awe my gf said you were so tense the whole movie. I didn't realize i had been death gripping both arms of my chair the entire movie!!

I went back to watch it two more times with different buddies... my only regret is not driving two towns over to the closest iMax and seeing it on the monster screen. 

I'm a huge movie buff (movie nerd lol) have been since I was little... this movie blew my mind!!!
4. It was insane for the 13yr old me 
5. It was absolutely incredible, epic as hell. I was completely blown away watching it in theaters. The next day I took my parents to see it too because I wanted them to be just as stunned, and they totally were.
  - I’ll always remember this because it was the first time my big sister took me out of our town to see a film. 

And we were blown away, all we saw was a tv spot sometime in that week and was just like, yeah we gotta see what this is.

Then we missed the last train home and had to take buses and randomly her friend turned up out of nowhere and helped us get home. We just talked about crazily different it was from anything else at that time.


6. "It does look like rain."

\*Epic metal guitar reverbs around the theater\*
7. Incredible 

## 4. I hope Dune part 3 finally makes people feel bad for rooting for paul (Timothee Chalamet).

**Score:** 614
**Body:** Probably half of the people walked out of part two thinking paul was a total badass,>!but the reality is he’s basically responsible for a galactic genocide.!<i really hope villeneuve doesn't "hollywood-ify" the third movie. it should be uncomfortable to watch. if half the audience doesn’t leave the theater feeling>!betrayed by their "hero",!<then we didn't get a real adaptation of messiah.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Everyone? Even on first watch I was amazed by him but not cause I'd think he's a badass. Paul became a model cult leader and movie ends with him leading his fanatic followers into jihad ffs. His story is a tragedy and even he himself knows it, his visions clearly showed him that if he becomes Kwisatz Haderach it will lead to catastrophe, all that remains is seeing that hell he foresaw unfold. On one hand I do expect a spectacle, but as same time I fully know that what we can expect is tragedy, not some glorious victory.

When I praise Paul's character for being charismatic in scenes like the one in council where he finally promises Fremen he'll lead them to green paradise, I don't do it cause I think he's in right, I just praise the acting performance and writing for actually giving the audience a taste of how charismatic cult leader can feel to people under their influence. I had goosebumps in those scenes, I won't lie about that, I felt mesmerized, and that's the thing about it, I can recognize what he's doing as terribly wrong, but as movie enjoyer I can fully embrace it in present moment and ride the wave of atmosphere.
2. Agreed. 

Also there are no heroes in Herbert’s universe. Nobody you can feel for, just schemers and assassins and victims. 

And occasionally space tigers 🐯 

P.S. also also: the Baron is a great villain, with no redeeming qualities, but he dies too FAST. 

It’s great in the novel [idk how to make SPOILERS happen so won’t say more] but over too fast and doesn’t feel like enough, after all the harm he’s caused.
  - I would disagree.  Leto II Atreides is portrayed as pretty heroic from the audience's point of view (obviously in-universe he's the most despised Altreides of all).  There's a dichotomy being drawn as he shows Leto as the man >!who is willing to sacrifice and do what he hates for the good of mankind and is hated vs Paul who flinches and runs and yet is beloved by the Fremen but for all the wrong reasons.!<
    - How do you do that SPOILERS thing?

I wanna elaborate on something from the books, but don’t wanna over-spoil for folks who have only seen the movies or adaptations. 
      - Like this but no spaces > ! SPOILER HERE ! < 

>!Like so ☺️!<
  - I dunno, I feel bad for Leto Atreides. At least in the film, he seems like a good and decent man and father who ends up dead because of his own sense of honor and duty.
I have only seen the films I haven't read the books. 
3. ‘Paul is actually the bad guy’ always felt like such a reductive take on Dune. 
  - RIght. It's more complicated than that.
  - Paul is a grey character which makes him so much more interesting.
4. Wait, he did the right thing right ?

weighing between many terrible outcomes and possibilities he picks the best one? the golden path based on his premonitions? 


## 5. Colin Farrell and Brendan Gleeson in In Bruges are amazing. Their dynamic is absolute perfection.

**Score:** 528
**Body:** Rewatched this gem recently and the chemistry between these two is so palpable. 

Their banter, quick exchanges and emotional moments are just spot on. Their friendship is so believable!

One of my favorite dynamic duos and my favorite of McDonagh’s flicks!

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Both should have won Oscars for Banshees of Inisherin 
  - This. And Kerry Condon was amazing as well.
    - I love Kerry Condon 
  - Yes and yes.

Farrell and Gleason are an amazing duo. The scene where Colm tells Padraic he’s not looking to be friends is just incredible.
2. One of a kind film. Never seen comedy and drama moulded together so well. A classic 
  - It works so well! McDonagh has a knack for writing offbeat, quirky characters but making them so believable.
3. The tower/elephant scene makes me cry. Sometimes i quote it with my mates and still laugh so hard about it.
4. "And I realized, fuck man, maybe that's what hell is: the entire rest of eternity spent in fuckin' Bruges. And I really really hoped I wouldn't die. I really really hoped I wouldn't die."
  - That monologue was just perfect. It had the right blend of dark humor and tragedy. 

Farrell is an amazing actor. 
5. One normal beer for me because I am normal. This is the life 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Sinners and it’s incredibly clumsy, half-baked second act

**Score:** 660
**Body:** Whenever people criticise Sinners, a lot of the critiques seem to suggest that the movie falls apart in it’s third act, in the battle between the humans and the vampires. And while I have my own gripes with the third act, to me, the most problematic part of the movie is the second act: after the Vampires are revealed. 

Even by the standards of any horror film, the second-act has always stood out to me as being clumsy and half-baked. The entire second act feels incredibly low in tension, and Coogler seems to have very little ideas about how to fill up the runtime than to repeat a bunch of scenes of people asking to be let inside the juke joint. 

When I first watched the movie in cinemas, I got whiplash when the climax kicked in, because I simply couldn’t believe that the entire second act was wasted on alternating between scenes of Vampires asking to be let inside, and some menial conversations between the survivors, most of which feel like afterthoughts. Sammy, who is the heart of the first half, might as well be relegated into a supporting character in the second act. For a movie that has such a throbbing heart in the first and third act, the second act has always stood out to me as being lethargic and low on any movement or kinetic energy.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I mostly really liked the movie, but I was annoyed at how dumb it seemed to think I was. It opens with narration about how some people can play music that bridges that transcends time, then repeats that narration during the "I Lied to You" scene in case we forgot. When Annie's dying and says "I've got someone waiting for you" it flashes back to their child's grave. Like dude, I'm following along just fine thanks.
  - They also did the flashbacks to the brothers buying the property when they reveal they were set up by the klan.

I also thought the epilogue was a really clumsy way to spell out the themes when the movie did a great job showing them already
    - A bunch of scenes for stupid people, essentially. It's embarrassing how much this happens in modern films, I'm tired of them clearly being made in a boardroom and some suit making sure idiots and people on their phones can follow along.
  - YES. I felt exactly this way too. I like Coogler but he treats his audience like we’re idiots. 
    - Not written like that in the script. It’s likely product of testing the film and dumb audiences not understanding clearly. 
    - I feel like these things are habits from making MCU movies 
    - It’s the always on their phone people.  The movies have to play well in the theater.  And then on streaming, people with the attention span of squirrels need the constant reminder of plot points.
2. I really was hoping for more as well. Especially when it came to the connection between the church, sin, music, and demons. I was hoping for more of Sammy’s connection with religion and his pastor father. And it was framed like that in the beginning too when Sammy is rushing into his father’s congregation, implying this would be an important theme by the end. 

The KKK plot came out of almost nowhere and left me cold. I think they couldn’t figure out how to connect the themes at the end. 
  - Yeah the movie sets up in its prologue AND in its standout sequence that music can open a doorway between the veil and connect the last, present and future. And spends so much time setting up Sammie as having a gift that seems almost God-given, but instead of using it in his father’s church, he’s using it for a very different kind of spiritual experience.

So clearly the film is setting up a battle that uses the supernatural quality of his music for the end climax right? Especially after showing the vampires also having a spiritual connection to music and ability to share each other’s memories and culture, right?

Oh no, the end is just a sloppy fight scene with questionable character choices, poorly choreographed action and a needlessly bloody massacre of the KKK tacked on for some reason. What a letdown.

The best spectacle films use things like musical/action sequences to both further the plot and tell you about our characters. This film does neither. And it jams so many ideas in there that it doesn’t know what to do with them, so just abandons them in favour of a very unsatisfying genre finale. Which is especially egregious, because there is a rich history of horror films that use the “overnight siege by supernatural forces” trope far better and more compellingly tense.

Instead, this film goes out of its way to undermine its own stakes. Passing-around-the-garlic sequence to find out who is the secret vampire, similar to the infamous sequence in The Thing must be similarly tense right? Oh wait, no one’s a vampire, so no.

The twins having double-crossed both sides in Chicago is surely setting up a reckoning where angry gangsters come after them, right? Nope, never becomes an issue.

Even relegating the KKK attack to some weird addendum that you could cut out of the film to literally no sequence sucks the dramatic possibilities out of the story. Why not have the KKK come after them during the middle of the night, to burn down the barn and cause maximum casualties? Then the vampires could attack them, and you have supernaturally powered KKK dudes. Or they could have a three-way battle? Pretty much any other option would be far more intense and dramatic than anything the film attempts.
3. "there are no 3rd act problems, only 1st act problems "  -- some class I took.

Its a shame because there are so many rich cool ideas in this film.  Its like looking at amazing ingredients for a gourmet meal  ground into a slurry and deep fried.  The story is not plotted.  We meet every character and learn all details of every conflict in the first act.  The story would have been better served by following A character or A single story from start to finish, introducing the addl't chars and subplots along the way. A good plotting has these various threads intersect at key story points.  Instead they are all bundled togther like a merrygoroudn rolling down a hill.  And I kept waiting for the Chi Mob to show up for their money.  Vampires vs Tommy Guns, baby.

## 2. Have you ever seen a movie from the 90s called A Simple Plan?

**Score:** 80
**Body:** I somehow missed it at the time. Coming out in 1998, this film by Sam Raimi was well-reviewed, but maybe went under the radar compared to higher-profile films released that year.

I watched it last night for the first time. What a dad-gum masterpiece. It stars Bill Paxton, Billie Bob Thorton, Bridget Fonda, and others.

I was completely riveted, moved, and disturbed by the end. Without going into spoilers, it’s a rural crime story, involving three characters who discover a small plane crash in the woods, containing a bag filled with millions of dollars.

I would say it has similarities with Fargo, released a couple years earlier, but this film is perhaps even more bleak and sad, and certainly stands on its own.

The story unfolds with a relentlessly precise logic, but feels completely human and psychologically true at the same time.

Check it out if you haven’t seen it. I feel like it deserves more attention than it has. A real under appreciated gem.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Love it, seen it a few times. I'm 60, so I'm sure I watched it the first time as a rental from the local video store not long after it came out. I kind of associate it with Raimi's The Gift somehow, I guess since they came out pretty close together.
  - Not only did Raimi direct both movies, but Billy Bob Thornton co-wrote The Gift. IIRC, his mother was a fortune teller, and he based it on that.
  - I group A simple Plan with Affliction (1997), another small-town neo-noir film by Paul Schraeder and starring Nick Nolte, and the lesser known In the Bedroom (2001) directed by Todd Field and starring Sissy Spacek. If you liked Fargo and Simple Plan, then these films are worth checking out.
    - In the Bedroom was a low-key hit - the budget was $1.7 million and it grossed $23 million in the U.S., and it got five Oscar nominations.  It got a proper release and campaign by Miramax.

The other two didn't get much of a push from their distributor and lost money.  Despite that, James Coburn won an Oscar (and Nolte got nominated) for Affliction, and A Simple Plan also got two nominations.

These are three really good movies, and none of them depend on car chases, explosions, or beautiful women uncrossing their legs when sitting in a chair.  Character-driven stories done very well.
2. I saw it at the cinema when it came out. Loved it then, rewatched it last year, still love it.

It's one of the best "money is the source of evil" moral thrillers ever made for my money, up there with The Treasure of Sierra Madre. Billy Bob's character is so well done... the careful peeling back of layers to him when he at first seems like a bit of a caricature is incredible.

When it came out, I distinctly remember some people dismissing it as a bit of a Fargo-knockoff, which is a shame because I think it's a very different film that stands on its own merits. I think the timing of its release probably hurt it in that regard, plus also Raimi at the time was only really known for the Evil Dead movies and (now cult films but flops at the time) Darkman and The Quick and the Dead. This is of course his most restrained film, but I think people were either dismissing of him as a more sober director, or went in expecting all hell to break loose in a Grand Guignol-way, which of course it doesn't really.

Just such a quietly effective, devastating film.
  - It’s Billy Bob’s character I can’t stop thinking about. He is so well-drawn and specific, and just pitiful. Even though he says and does stupid things, it’s hard to call him stupid. He’s utterly naive, but sincere and ultimately good-hearted, and you understand him completely.
3. Can confirm. A real gem that slipped under most people’s radar. 

I see the Fargo comparisons, but yeah, this one’s much more cynical, IMO. Less of a good vs. evil and much more of a “human beings are pretty much terrible by nature” pic, IIRC. (It’s been a while.) Margie is not there to ground and humanize the awful happenings. 

Also, surprisingly strong work from Bridget Fonda, of whom I’m generally not terribly Fond-a. (*ahem*… sorry.)

It’s obviously not as influential as the Evil Dead films, but I think it’s arguably Raimi’s best work. Very steady, great pacing, slowly cranking up the tension. 
4. I've said it elsewhere on Reddit, but Billy Bob Thornton's performance in A Simple Plan is one of my all-time favorites. I've seen him in plenty of other things (but oddly, never Sling Blade), and he always come across as intelligent. But in ASP, he turns off every bit of intelligence from behind his eyes. He's just so believably...dumb.
5. So funny. I have to tell you. Dead serious I just bought the Blu Ray on Arrow video yesterday and have spent the entire day telling my friends about how good the film is. And how we should watch this. And then I see this post. 


It's a great film.
6. Just watched it for the first time two weeks ago. Absolute banger of a thriller. Great performances from everyone, but what really struck me was the cinematography. Raimi's movies usually have that hyperactive and highly stylized vibe to them, which plays to his strength as a campy horror director. But A Simple Plan is completely the opposite; it's restrained, austere and deliberate. It might be my favorite film from him.

## 3. Babylon is an underrated masterpiece

**Score:** 25
**Body:** I recently watched Babylon and I was in complete awe for the entirety of its 3 hour runtime. I think this film is the greatest epic made in a decade and I’m honestly shocked by the lower reviews it has. The film managed to capture a sense of urgency and crackerjack thrills that reminded me of WoWST.  I had always been a fan of Damien Chazelle but this really took him to a whole nother level in my eyes.  The way he was able to combine pathos, comedy, satire, period authenticity and social/ historical commentary was honestly pretty astonishing imo. 

Whiplash has always been one of my favorite films but I think in terms of what Babylon managed to say and reveal while still being fun the entire time was nothing short of miraculous. The plot lines surrounding Sidney Palmer and the charcoal, Nellie Laroy’s character arc, Jack Conrad and the movie critic, and Manny firing the Asian dancer, then him going from breaking up a strike at the start to running out of the billionaires mansion and coming to terms with his relationship to the cinema and finding his community were behaviorally and socially insightful in ways that movies rarely are. 

The storyline with Sidney Palmer being forced to do blackface and then deciding it wasn’t worth it and going to play in a community theater was one of the most impactful things I’ve seen. It gave me a newfound respect for Chazelle and he seems to be a lot smarter than I had given him credit for before. 

Marty Supreme, Frankenstein and No Other Choice were my fav films from this year but I feel Babylon was astronomically better than all of them. It’s instantly joined my favorite films of all time list after.

Did anyone else enjoy this film as much as me? I’m surprised it wasn’t more popular as I genuinely can’t find many faults with the film other than maybe its brashness which was honestly a major plus for me. 

Any thoughts?

4. It is not a masterpiece at all. The movie is a almost a scene for scene copy of Boogie nights, but replacing the porn story with a copy of Singing in the Rains story. 

But where Boogie Nights is a brilliant character study, Chazelle replaced those beats with longer party scenes (that again replace character with with excess and spectacle. The party scenes in Boogie nights always had a point dramatically other than "isn't this wild??? Isn't it???")

So you get to the end and you get Margot Robbies character struggling to get work and you have to have another character say "no one will hire your because of your bad gambling problems". And you the viewer are left to wonder "what gambling problems?". Where Boogie nights shows you the descent into drugs, Babylon just had a character say "hey, that gambling problem if yours is really hurting you".

As was the enormous effort they had to go through to capture Nellie just walking into her mark and saying one line, the amount of effort it took to make the sound work— I loved it!

Margot is great, Pitt is awesome, Diego Calva crushed it and the Tobey McGuire part was weird and disturbing but worked for me. 

It’s kind of a mess of a movie and all over the board but I think it will age well and people will go back to it. 

## 4. Yi Yi (2000) Review - My first Edward Yang movie!

**Score:** 19
**Body:** Starts from a marriage and ends at a funeral... covers everything in between and more!  
Little Yang Yang says that we can only see half the truth because we can't see what others see, and we can't even see the back of our heads. So, he innocently starts giving people pictures of the backs of their heads!  
We often strive to know the truth - of ourselves, of our surroundings, of our loved ones. Yet we forget that the reality we live in is not entirely objective. There are objective realities, and there are subjective realities - what we feel, what we think of others and so on. We can never know or experience others' subjective realities. We can just try to relate with what they tell or show us, or we can try to empathize based on our own subjective realities.  
And for our own subjective realities, we often go through them without realising why or how we are stuck with them. Why can't we be happy, and what seems to be dragging us down? Why are we filled with such sadness and grief?  
Those shots with the camera looking at a character in grief in a dark room through a glass with reflections of a well-lit, continuously moving city encapsulated this feeling so well for me. And I loved the slow pacing of the movie, which I usually do.  
As Fatty said, movies like these allow us to experience twice as much life as in reality.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I’m positive that Yi Yi is the best movie that I have ever seen. Its technical prowess is simply unmatched. The story is familiar yet entirely captivating. The style is mundane yet astonishingly stunning. I can understand that this may not strike the same chord for everyone, but there is no doubt in my mind that Yi Yi is a timeless masterpiece that will continue to shape lives and cinema forever.
  - Really! It connected with me so well. It was one of those surreal experiences when you suddenly realise that the movie you are watching will be one of your all time favourites forever!
2. Yang Yang is one of my absolute favourite film characters of all time. His ‘conversation’ with his grandma at the end of the film had me absolutely balling my eyes out. Such a masterpiece, top 5 film of the 21st century for me
3. I had a really similar experience with Yi Yi, it kind of sneaks up on you emotionally instead of hitting you all at once.

That idea about only seeing part of the truth really stuck with me, too, especially how the film keeps shifting perspectives between characters. It makes even small moments feel layered, like you’re constantly aware there’s something you’re not fully seeing.

those reflection shots you mentioned stayed with me after as well. It’s such a quiet way of showing how disconnected people can feel, even when everything around them is moving forward.

It’s one of those films that made me slow down a bit while watching, and somehow that made everything feel more real rather than less.
4. I want to like Yi Yi more and intend to rewatch it, but my first Edward Yang was A Brighter Summer Day. By comparison, ABSD is bigger, darker, and was completely enveloping to me. So maybe my expectations were just off.
  - I kind of had the same experience with Yi Yi after ABSD. The whole thing was such a journey and tipped off perfectly by that ending. I’m not usually a huge fan of super long films like that but ABSD is up in my top 5. I do find it interesting that Yi Yi gets a lot more attention and finds itself on more “Greatest films of all time” lists. Maybe I just wasn’t in the right headspace when I saw it.
    - Really looking forward to ABSD!
    - ABSD is my favorite argument for “good movies could always be longer, bad movies could always be shorter”. Yeah it’s a huge 4 hour movie, but by the end you’re swimming in it, completely different than watching a 2 hr movie.
5. The scene that juxtaposes Yang-Yang learning to swim and Ting-Ting having the final conversation with her grandmother changed my whole brain chemistry in relation to film and film-making. Can't remember why I ever watched Yi Yi in the first place but it it almost single-handedly changed my whole taste on movies 

## 5. DVD with Silent Meshes of the Afternoon

**Score:** 7
**Body:** I know I’m getting into the weeds of collecting now, because this is a pretty specific ask 😆

I have the Maya Deren Collection from Kino and it’s my favorite DVD in my collection — BUT I am bothered that the only version of Meshes of the Afternoon is the revised version with Teiji Ito’s score. Does anyone know if there is a DVD or Blu-Ray that has the original version of Meshes of the Afternoon, with the three extra shots and no soundtrack? Maybe in a collection of early experimental films of pioneer women directors or something? I remember this version was briefly on the Criterion Channel, but I don’t know what their source was.

For what it’s worth I think Ito’s score is great and enjoy the sound version, but in my opinion the silent film is infinitely superior (but that’s a conversation for another time!)
